original_abstract:
The energy-intensive nature of cryogenic distillation for propylene/propane separation necessitates the development of more sustainable alternatives, such as adsorptive separation using metal–organic frameworks (MOFs). While molecular sieving provides high selectivity by size exclusion, achieving high adsorption capacity remains a significant technical barrier. In this study, we report the rational design of UTSA-400, an oxyfluoride-based ultramicroporous MOF ([Ni(WO$_2$F$_4$)(pyz)$_2$]), engineered to optimize guest binding affinity for propylene while completely excluding propane. 

By substituting traditional pillar ligands with the oxofluorotungstate WO$_2$F$_4^{2–}$ anion, we precisely tuned the pore environment to enhance gas–host interactions. Molecular electrostatic potential mapping and structural analysis reveal that the exposed oxide/fluoride pairs within the one-dimensional channels serve as potent binding sites. Consequently, UTSA-400 exhibits a substantial increase in propylene uptake—exceeding its niobate analog by 544% at 0.1 bar—and achieves a packing density (0.93 g cm$^{–3}$) that surpasses the density of liquid propylene. Experimental in situ infrared spectroscopy and dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT-D) calculations confirm that a combination of strong hydrogen bonding and maximized van der Waals contacts underlies this enhanced affinity. 

Dynamic breakthrough experiments demonstrate that UTSA-400 can directly produce polymer-grade (>99.5%) propylene from equimolar mixtures with high productivity. Furthermore, the framework exhibits excellent stability under air, water, and varying pH conditions. This work highlights how atomic-level pore engineering and the regulation of framework dynamics can overcome the traditional capacity–selectivity trade-off in molecular sieving applications.
humanized_abstract:
Cryogenic distillation for propylene/propane separation requires significant energy, so there is a strong motivation to develop more sustainable alternatives. An attractive alternative is adsorptive separation based on metal–organic frameworks (MOFs). Although molecular sieving affords remarkable selectivity based solely on size exclusion, the challenge of achieving sufficient adsorption capacity has so far impeded practical application. Here, we report the synthesis of a highly specific adsorbent, UTSA-400, the oxyfluoride-based ultramicroporous Ni MOF, [Ni(WO$_2$F$_4$)(pyz)$_2$], a material that we have rationally engineered with precisely the right affinity for propylene adsorption and the precise selectivity of completely excluding propane.

Upon substitution of the common pillar ligands with the oxofluorotungstate anion, WO2F42–, we obtained a material with an exceptionally controlled pore environment, and therefore gas–host interactions. Potential mapping, combined with detailed structural information show that the oxide/fluoride pairs on the surface of the one-dimensional channels function as highly desirable gas adsorption sites. Indeed, UTSA-400 has a higher propylene uptake than its niobate counterpart by 544% at 0.1 bar and also possesses the highest packing density value of 0.93 g cm–3 that is even higher than that of liquid propylene at ambient temperature. The adsorption mechanism, as derived from experimental in situ FTIR spectra, is not only explained by hydrogen bonding interactions between the propylene and the fluoride ligands in the channel along with the surface-located oxide pairs, but also validated using dispersion-corrected density functional theory (DFT-D) calculations, where both the hydrogen bonding interactions and the enhanced van der Waals between UTSA-400 and propylene contribute to the highly efficient adsorption behavior.

Dynamic breakthrough experiments show that UTSA-400 can catalytically convert equimolar feed mixtures into polymer-grade (>99.5%) propylene with high productivity under dynamic conditions. Furthermore, UTSA-400 shows high stability under various conditions in air, water, and across a range of pH values. This work shows for the first time at the atomic level how precise control of the pore architecture in a material can allow for the dynamic regulation of the framework structure in such a way as to resolve the long-standing capacity-selectivity trade-off inherent in molecular sieving, and highlights the huge potential in advancing the field of zeolite-based catalysis.

document.output:
Cryogenic distillation for propylene/propane separation requires significant energy, so there is a strong motivation to develop more sustainable alternatives. An attractive alternative is adsorptive separation based on metal–organic frameworks (MOFs). Although molecular sieving affords remarkable selectivity based solely on size exclusion, the challenge of achieving sufficient adsorption capacity has so far impeded practical application. Here, we report the synthesis of a highly specific adsorbent, UTSA-400, the oxyfluoride-based ultramicroporous Ni MOF, [Ni(WO$_2$F$_4$)(pyz)$_2$], a material that we have rationally engineered with precisely the right affinity for propylene adsorption and the precise selectivity of completely excluding propane.

Upon substitution of the common pillar ligands with the oxofluorotungstate anion, WO2F42–, we obtained a material with an exceptionally controlled pore environment, and therefore gas–host interactions. Potential mapping, combined with detailed structural information show that the oxide/fluoride pairs on the surface of the one-dimensional channels function as highly desirable gas adsorption sites. Dynamic breakthrough experiments show that UTSA-400 can catalytically convert equimolar feed mixtures into polymer-grade (>99.5%) propylene with high productivity under dynamic conditions. Furthermore, UTSA-400 shows high stability under various conditions in air, water, and across a range of pH values. This work shows for the first time at the atomic level how precise control of the pore architecture in a material can allow for the dynamic regulation of the framework structure in such a way as to resolve the long-standing capacity-selectivity trade-off inherent in molecular sieving, and highlights the huge potential in advancing the field of zeolite-based catalysis.
document.input:
The energy-intensive nature of cryogenic distillation for propylene/propane separation necessitates the development of more sustainable alternatives, such as adsorptive separation using metal–organic frameworks (MOFs). While molecular sieving provides high selectivity by size exclusion, achieving high adsorption capacity remains a significant technical barrier.
